title: Happy indeed is the person Who has nothing of one's own. One of perfect knowledge, Having nothing of one's own. Behold the affliction Of those…
content:
Happy indeed is the person
Who has nothing of one's own.
One of perfect knowledge,
Having nothing of one's own.

Behold the affliction
Of those who have possessions,
Which causes one person
To be bound to another.

title: - Whatever is dependent, that is agitated. Whatever is not dependent, that is not agitated. With no agitation, there is tranquility. With tranquility, there is…
content: - Whatever is dependent, that is agitated. Whatever is not dependent, that is not agitated. With no agitation, there is tranquility. With tranquility, there is no inclination. With no inclination, there is no coming and going. With no coming and going, there is no passing away and arising. With no passing away and arising, there is no this world, no next world, no intermediate state. This is the end of suffering.

title: Just as, brahmin, a blue lotus, or a red lotus, or a white lotus, born in the water, grown up in the water, rises up…
content: Just as, brahmin, a blue lotus, or a red lotus, or a white lotus, born in the water, grown up in the water, rises up above the water and stands without being stained by the water, so too, brahmin, I was born in the world, grown up in the world, yet having conquered the world, I dwell untainted by the world. Brahmin, remember me as the Buddha.

title: Friends, what is the development of concentration that, when developed and frequently practiced, leads to blissful abiding here and now? Here, friends, a bhikkhu, secluded…
content: Friends, what is the development of concentration that, when developed and frequently practiced, leads to blissful abiding here and now? Here, friends, a bhikkhu, secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and abides in the first jhāna ... second jhāna ... third jhāna ... enters and abides in the fourth jhāna. Friends, this is the development of concentration that, when developed and frequently practiced, leads to blissful abiding here and now.

title: Wandering alone, a sage, heedful, Unmoved by praise or blame, Like a lion unstartled by sounds, Like the wind not caught in a net, Like…
content:
Wandering alone, a sage, heedful,
Unmoved by praise or blame,
Like a lion unstartled by sounds,
Like the wind not caught in a net,
Like a lotus unstained by water —
A leader of others, not led by others —
The wise know that one as a sage.

One who acts like a bath-pillar when others speak extremely,
Without craving, with well-controlled faculties —
The wise know that one as a sage.

title: And, Ānanda, what is the covering over with grass? Here, Ānanda, some bhikkhus live quarrelling, disputing, and arguing, having done and said many things unbecoming…
content: And, Ānanda, what is the covering over with grass? Here, Ānanda, some bhikkhus live quarrelling, disputing, and arguing, having done and said many things unbecoming to samaṇas. All those bhikkhus should assemble in harmony. Having assembled, a more learned and experienced bhikkhu from one group of bhikkhus rises from his seat, arranges his robe over one shoulder, joins his palms, and addresses the Saṅgha: 'Venerable sirs, let the Saṅgha hear me. When we were living quarrelling, disputing, and arguing, we did and said many things unbecoming to samaṇas. If it seems proper to the Saṅgha, I will reveal whatever offenses these venerable ones have and whatever offenses I have. For the benefit of the venerable ones and also for my own benefit, I will reveal them in the midst of the Saṅgha, like covering over with grass, except for the grave offenses and offenses connected with laypeople.'

title: But one who has boarded a strong boat, well-fitted with oars and rudder, Skilled and mindful, knowing the means, can ferry many across. So too,…
content:
But one who has boarded a strong boat, well-fitted with oars and rudder,
Skilled and mindful, knowing the means, can ferry many across.
So too, one who is developed, learned, unshaken,
Can help others who are meditating, who are eager to hear the Dhamma.

Therefore, associate with a good person, a wise one, learned and intelligent.
Knowing the goal, practicing wisely, knowing the Dhamma, one attains happiness.

title: And, bhikkhus, what is the practice of endurance? Here, bhikkhus, a person does not revile back at one who has reviled, does not get angry…
content: And, bhikkhus, what is the practice of endurance? Here, bhikkhus, a person does not revile back at one who has reviled, does not get angry at one who has become angry, does not quarrel with one who has quarreled. This, bhikkhus, is called the practice of endurance.

title: Therefore, Māluṅkyaputta, remember what I have left unanswered as unanswered, and what I have answered as answered. And what, Māluṅkyaputta, have I left unanswered? 'The…
content: Therefore, Māluṅkyaputta, remember what I have left unanswered as unanswered, and what I have answered as answered. And what, Māluṅkyaputta, have I left unanswered? 'The world is eternal' — I have left unanswered. 'The world is not eternal' — I have left unanswered. 'The world is finite' — I have left unanswered. 'The world is infinite' — I have left unanswered. 'The soul and the body are the same' — I have left unanswered. 'The soul and the body are different' — I have left unanswered. 'The Tathāgata exists after death' — I have left unanswered. 'The Tathāgata does not exist after death' — I have left unanswered. And why have I left these unanswered? Because they are not connected with the goal, they are not fundamental to the holy life, they do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. Therefore I have left them unanswered. And what, Māluṅkyaputta, have I answered? 'This is suffering' — I have answered. 'This is the origin of suffering' — I have answered. 'This is the cessation of suffering' — I have answered. 'This is the path leading to the cessation of suffering' — I have answered. And why have I answered these? Because they are connected with the goal, they are fundamental to the holy life, they lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. Therefore I have answered them.

title: Venerable sirs, if a bhikkhu, examining thus, knows: 'I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much…
content: Venerable sirs, if a bhikkhu, examining thus, knows: 'I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much overcome by dullness and drowsiness; I dwell much with restlessness; I dwell much with doubt; I dwell much with anger; I dwell much with a defiled mind; I dwell much with bodily fever; I dwell much with laziness; I dwell much with an unconcentrated mind,' then that bhikkhu should arouse determination, desire, energy, effort, exertion, non-backsliding, mindfulness, and clear comprehension to abandon those evil unwholesome states. Just as, venerable sirs, when a cloth is on fire or the head is on fire, one should arouse determination, desire, energy, effort, exertion, non-backsliding, mindfulness, and clear comprehension to extinguish the burning cloth or head. So too, venerable sirs, that bhikkhu should arouse determination, desire, energy, effort, exertion, non-backsliding, mindfulness, and clear comprehension to abandon those evil unwholesome states. Venerable sirs, if a bhikkhu, examining thus, knows: 'I dwell much with a mind without greed; I dwell much with a mind without hatred; I dwell much with a mind without dullness and drowsiness; I dwell much with a mind without restlessness; I dwell much with a mind beyond doubt; I dwell much with a mind without anger; I dwell much with an undefiled mind; I dwell much with a body without fever; I dwell much with an energetic and striving mind; I dwell much with a concentrated mind,' then that bhikkhu, having established himself in those wholesome states, should further focus on the destruction of the taints.
